segments.s321-g49: نهانی functions as a noun/adjective 'a secret' (or 'something secret') and heads a relative clause introduced by که. که is the relative 'that/which'. دارد + subjunctive بگوید is a light-verb construction meaning 'has (something) to say' (literally 'has that he should say'); بگوید here is subjunctive/infinitival in sense 'to say/tell'. به ما marks the intended addressee 'to us'.
segments.s321-g50: همان 'that very/the same' modifies چاره 'remedy/solution'. آن is a demonstrative pronoun 'that' (referring back to the secret/remedy). بجوید is 3rd-person subjunctive/jussive 'let him seek' (used to issue a recommendation or command); ز = 'from' and ما = 'us' mark the source/agent from whom the remedy should be sought.
segments.s321-g51: بدیشان is the prepositional object 'to them' (به ایشان contracted). چنین گفت = 'said thus' (چنین 'thus' + گفت past 3rd sg.). پس is a temporal/discourse adverb 'then' placed before the subject شهریار 'the king' — word order in Persian allows adverbial/objects before the verb, so the whole reads 'Then the king said to them thus.'
segments.s321-g52: که introduces the reported content 'that ...'. با کس means 'with anyone/with any person' (با + کس). ندارید is present 2nd-person-plural 'you do not have'. کس کارزار is a nominal phrase ‘a person for the battle / a man for fighting’ (کس here is a noun 'person'); the clause means 'that you have no one to engage in battle with.' Note the double appearance of کس: the first marks the prepositional complement (with anyone), the second is the noun 'person' in the predicate.
segments.s321-g53: به گیتی is a locative phrase 'in the world'. ز دشمن 'from the enemy' marks source of suffering. مرا (here split as م + را) is the first‑person object 'to/for me' (هست/نیست construction attaches experiencer). نیست is the negative existential 'is not'. رنج is the subject 'suffering/hardship.' The clause means 'There is no suffering for me from the enemy in the world.'
segments.s321-g54: نشد is past tense 'did not happen / was not' (here 'there was not'). نیز 'also/too' links this clause to the previous clause negatively. جایی 'anywhere/some place'. پراکنده 'scattered' modifies گنج 'treasure.' Together: 'nor was there any place where treasure was scattered.'
segments.s321-g55: نه is negation 'no/not'. آزار 'harm/annoyance' is the object; دارم 'I have' is present 1st-singular. ز کار سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the army's affair/campaign' (ز = 'from', کار = 'affair/deed', سپاه = 'army'). The clause yields 'I have no harm from the army's campaign.'
segments.s321-g56: نه ... نه sets up a second negative clause. اندر شما 'among you' (اندر = in/among). هست is the existential/copula 'there is / is'. مرد گناه is a nominal phrase 'a man of guilt' meaning 'a guilty man' or 'a man at fault.' The clause means 'nor among you is there a man at fault.'
segments.s321-g89: همی گفت is a classical/perfective-continuous phrase: همی is a durative/continuative particle meaning “kept / was continually,” and گفت is the past-tense verb “said.” ای is the vocative particle “O!”; برتر از برتری is a doubled-comparative vocative: برتر (“superior”) + از used in the comparative sense “than” (here poetic), followed by the abstract noun برتری (“superiority”), producing a heightened epithet “higher than superiority.”
segments.s321-g90: فزاینده is a present-participle/adjectival form meaning “increasing” or “augmenting,” functioning here as an epithet modifying the following nouns. پاکی و مهتری are two coordinated nouns linked by و: پاکی (“purity”) and مهتری (a poetic noun meaning “preeminence / majesty”); together they complete the vocative description of the addressee.
segments.s321-g91: تو باشی is an optative/subjunctive imperative address: تو (you) + باشی (be) — poetically rendered as “be thou” or “may you be.” به مینو is a prepositional phrase “to/into the heaven” (مینو = heaven). م + را together represent the object clitic phrase مرا (“me” + object marker) placed before the noun for emphasis; رهنمای is a noun meaning “guide,” so مرا رهنمای = “(be) my guide.” The word order and clitic split are poetic.
segments.s321-g92: مگر here is an archaic/poetic particle introducing a hope or purpose clause (something like “that/perhaps” rather than its common meanings “except/unless”). بگذرم is first-person subjunctive/potential of گذشتن “to pass,” literally “that I may pass.” ز این سپنجی سرای is the prepositional phrase “from this spongy dwelling/house”: ز = from (poetic), این = this, سپنجی = adjectival ‘spongy’ (describing the body/house), سرای = “dwelling/house” (here poetic ‘abode’).
segments.s321-g93: نکردی is the negative past/perfect of کردن “to do/make” used here in a classical/poetic sense “did not do” or “has done nothing.” The subject/possessive phrase دلم (دل + enclitic م) “my heart” follows the verb (inverted poetic order). هیچ نایافته is a parenthetical/result phrase: هیچ “nothing/none” + نایافته (past-participle/adjective) “unattained / not found” — together indicating that the heart has found nothing.
segments.s321-g94: روان is the noun “soul/spirit.” جای روشن is a noun phrase “bright/clear place” (جای = place/position, روشن = bright/illuminated). دلان تافته is a genitival/attributive construction: دلان (plural of دل) “hearts,” modified by the past-participle تافته meaning “set apart / distinguished” (poetic), so دلان تافته = “hearts that are set apart/distinguished.” The whole segment sketches “the soul’s bright place for hearts that are distinguished.”
segments.s321-g9: This is a prepositional/partitive phrase headed by از (here 'from, out of') that links the following noun to the temporal phrase; آن ('that/those') specifies the group, نامداران is the plural noun 'the renowned ones', and روز نبرد is a genitive-like sequence 'the day of battle' (روز + نبرد = 'day of battle'). The whole chunk sets 'on/among those famed men, on the day of battle' as the scene for the next clause.
segments.s321-g10: همی is an archaic/poetic aspectual particle often used with past verbs to give iterative or continuous coloring ('kept on' / 'then'); هر کسی = 'each/every person'; دیگر here functions adverbially as 'otherwise/differently'; اندیشه کرد is a past tense verb phrase 'thought' (اندیشه + the light past auxiliary کرد). Together: 'each person then thought differently.'
segments.s321-g11: چو introduces a temporal clause ('when'). بر تخت شد is an idiomatic verbal phrase: بر + تخت + شد (شد = past/inchoative 'became/was seated') giving 'was seated on / ascended the throne.' نامور شهریار is the subject/predicate nominal 'the famed sovereign' placed after the verb—classical Persian permits verb before subject in such clauses.
segments.s321-g12: بیامد is the simple past 'came.' به marks direction/place ('to/into'), درگاه is 'the court/royal presence,' and سالار بار is a compound title: سالار 'commander/master' and بار 'load/cargo' (here 'baggage' or 'burden'), so 'the master/commander of the baggage' — i.e., a court official. The phrase as a whole: 'he came to the court of the chief of the baggage.'
segments.s321-g13: بفرمود is an honorific past verb meaning 'he commanded/said (authoritatively)'. تا here functions as a conjunction introducing the content of the order ('that/so that'), پرده is 'the curtain,' and برداشتند is past plural 'they removed/took away.' So: 'He commanded that they remove the curtain.'
segments.s321-g14: سپه 'the army' is marked by the object/topic marker را, then ز درگاه ('from/out of the court') gives the source/place, and بگذاشتند is past plural meaning here 'they left/placed (aside)'. Grammatically, را makes سپه definite/explicit as the object of بگذاشتند: 'they left the army outside the court.'
segments.s321-g15: برفتند is past plural 'they went off / they departed.' با دست کرده is a participial phrase literally 'having made (it) with the hand'—کرده is the perfect/participial form 'having done.' به کش means 'to the pull/draw' (به + کش 'to pull'), so the whole phrase describes men moving off 'with hands made to the draw' i.e. 'hands on their weapons' (a compact, idiomatic epic expression).
segments.s321-g16: This is a nominal string giving epithets of those who depart: بزرگان 'the great ones/chiefs' followed by two compound epithets formed by noun + agentive/participial element: پیل افکن 'elephant-thrower' (پیل + افکن 'one who casts/strikes elephants') and شیر فش 'lion-crusher/press' (شیر + فش). These are descriptive compounds common in epic diction.
segments.s321-g33: This clause uses the preposition به + noun phrase (به هر کشوری) to mean 'in/to every country/realm.' The copula ست (است) follows the possessive phrase لشکر و گنج تو to assert existence/possession: 'your army and treasure are (there).' Word order is typical Persian: locative/prepositional phrase first, then the possessed items, then the copula.
segments.s321-g34: به جایی که ... is a relative locative: 'to/at a place that...'. که introduces the relative clause. پی here means 'after/following' or 'wherever (one goes, something follows),' and برنهی is a poetic verbal form meaning roughly 'you set (your foot)/you go' or 'you put (yourself) upon' — together پی برنهی gives the sense 'wherever you go / wherever you set foot.' رنج توست ends the clause with the copula ست: 'your suffering is there / follows you.'
segments.s321-g35: ندانیم is the negative present form meaning 'we do not know' (ن + دانیم). کاندیشه is a possessive compound (کان + اندیشه) used here as 'the thought/intention,' and شهریار is the noun 'king'; together they form the object/topic of 'ندانیم' — 'we do not know the king's thought/intent.'
segments.s321-g36: چرا is the interrogative 'why.' تیره شد is a simple verbal phrase where تیره (ominous/gloomy) is the predicate adjective and شد is the past-tense/perfective 'became': 'has become gloomy.' اندر این روزگار is a locative temporal phrase 'in this time/age,' placing the cause or situation in the present era.
segments.s321-g37: ترا here is written as two tokens (ت + را) but functions together as the object 'to you' (ت is the enclitic 2nd-person object; را is the object/definite marker). ز این جهان روز برخوردنست is a poetic existential: ز + (این جهان) = 'from this world'; روز برخوردنست uses برخوردن + است to mean 'a day will befall/come'—so the whole means 'a day of departing from this world is appointed for you' (i.e., 'you will have a day of leaving this world').
segments.s321-g38: نه here negates the following predicate: 'it is not the time/occasion of ...' هنگام تیمار ('the time for care/solicitude') و پژمردنست ('and for withering/decline')—پژمردنست is the verbal noun/compound meaning 'to wither' with the copula, so the clause denies that this moment is for tending or for fading.
segments.s321-g39: گر is the conditional 'if.' از ما 'from us' marks the affected party. به چیزی ('by/with anything') introduces the manner/thing causing the harm. بیازرد is a verbal form here meaning 'he harms/afflicts' (3rd-person singular in the conditional clause) — so the clause reads 'If the king harms us by anything...' شاه names the agent 'the king.'
segments.s321-g40: از آزار او نیست ما را گناه literally arranges as 'from his harm there is not upon us guilt.' از آزار او ('from his harm') is the source phrase; نیست is the negative copula 'is not'; ما را combines the pronoun + object marker to mark 'on us' (i.e., 'our responsibility'); and گناه is the noun 'fault/blame.' The whole means 'his harming us is not our fault.'
segments.s321-g1: شب و روز یک هفته is an adverbial duration: 'day and night for a week.' بر پای بود is a past-tense predicate: بر (on/upon) + پای (foot/standing) + بود (past 'was'), here idiomatically meaning 'remained standing/was on his feet' or 'kept vigil.' Word order places the temporal phrase up front, then the verbal clause.
segments.s321-g2: تن آنجا and جانش دگر جای are two coordinated nominal phrases whose shared verb is the final بود ('was'). تن آنجا = 'his body (was) there.' جانش (جان + enclitic -ش 'his') = 'his life/soul'; دگر جای = 'another/other place.' The final بود applies to both coordinate halves.
segments.s321-g3: سر هفته را گشت is an idiomatic temporal clause: سر هفته (the head/turn of the week = 'the week's end/when the week came around') marked with را as topical/definite, + گشت (past 'turned/came about'). خسرو نوان follows as the resultant state: 'Khosrow (became) renewed/young' — word order places the time/clause first and the subject/predicate after the verb.
segments.s321-g4: به جای پرستش is a locative/prepositional phrase 'at/in the place of worship.' نماندش is the past negative of ماندن with enclitic -ش 'him' → 'did not remain for him' i.e. 'he did not have left.' توان is a noun 'strength/ability' placed after the verb in the idiom نماند(ه) توان = 'no strength remained.'
segments.s321-g5: به هشتم is a temporal phrase 'on the eighth (day).' ز جای پرستش = 'from the place of worship' (ز = poetic 'from'). برفت is an archaic/poetic past form meaning 'went away' (prefix بر- + رفت); here it functions as the main past verb 'he departed.'
segments.s321-g6: بر تخت شاهی is a prepositional phrase 'upon the royal throne' (بر = on, تخت = throne, شاهی = royal/kingship as adjective). خرامید is past 'he strode/strutted proudly' (third-person past). The final تفت is lexically unexpected in the supplied senses (it does not match the cooking senses given) and functions here either as an emphatic/poetic tag or a damaged/archaic form; its exact sense is not provided in the supplied list.
segments.s321-g7: همه پهلوانان ایران سپاه strings together a quantifier + noun + genitive: 'all the champions of Iran' with سپاه ('army/host') appositive or resumptive noun emphasizing that these champions form the army. Word order places the group first as topic.
segments.s321-g8: شگفتی فرومانده is a predicative phrase 'left in astonishment' (شگفتی = astonishment/wonder; فرومانده = past participle 'left/overcome'). از کار شاه is a causal phrase 'because of the king's deed' (از = because of; کار = deed/act; شاه = king). Together: 'left astonished because of the king's deed.'
segments.s321-g73: که introduces a relative clause (‘who/that’). او is the clause subject 'he'. داد is a past simple verb here ('gave/established') used transitively; داد بر + noun means 'set up/established over' or 'administered with respect to'. بر نیک و بد is a prepositional phrase 'over good and evil' (بر = over/against, نیک و بد = 'good and bad'), and دستگاه ('system/arrangement') is the governed noun meaning the institution or ordering established.
segments.s321-g74: ستایش is the noun 'praise'. مر is the speaker's pronoun 'me' (classical/formal order: 'praise of me' = 'praise from me' or 'my praise'). او را is the definite/direct-object phrase 'him' (را marks the definite object). که introduces a relative clause modifying او را; بنمود is the past verb 'showed' (poetic past of 'نمودن'), and راه is the object 'the way/road' shown.
segments.s321-g75: از آن پس is an adverbial phrase 'from that time on/after that'. به من marks the beneficiary 'for me'. شادمانی is the noun 'rejoicing/joy', and کنید is the (plural/formal) imperative/subjunctive 'do/make' (classical Persian uses کنید for polite commands), so the clause means 'rejoice for me' or 'make merriment on my behalf.'
segments.s321-g76: ز (poetic variant of از) introduces the source/thing to be removed: 'from evils' (بدها is plural 'evils/ills'). روان is 'the spirit/soul' as the thing affected. بی‌گمانی literally 'without doubt' functions adverbially/adjectivally meaning 'undoubting/assured'. کنید is again the imperative 'make/do' — together: 'remove from the soul the evils and make it free of doubt.'
segments.s321-g77: بدانید is the plural/imparative 'know' (an exhortation). کاین = 'that this' introduces the subordinate clause. چرخ here is the proverbial 'wheel' = fate/cycle (metaphorical). ناپایدار is an adjective 'unstable/transient' modifying چرخ, so the clause means 'know that this unstable wheel...' with the wheel as subject of the following predicate.
segments.s321-g78: نداند is the negative form 'does not know/does not deem/does not regard' — the subject (the wheel) is implied from the previous segment. همی here is an adverb 'always/still' emphasizing continuity. کهتر 'inferior' takes از + noun to mean 'less than' (کهتر از شهریار = 'less than the king'). The whole says the wheel does not always keep anyone inferior to the king (i.e., fortunes change).
segments.s321-g79: همی is the frequentative/emphatic adverb 'ever/always'. بدرود is used poetically as 'depart/part' (farewell), so 'همی بدرود' = 'continually departed'. پیر و برنا = 'the old and the young' (pēr = old, barna = young). به هم = 'together/with one another' (به + هم yields 'together'). The phrase describes repeated partings of both old and young.
segments.s321-g80: از او 'from him' (source/agent). داد here is the noun 'justice' (not the verb) — 'justice coming from him'. بینیم is the classical present/subjunctive form 'we see' used in narrative sense ('we saw/experienced'). و = and. زو (زو = از + او) again 'from him', هم = 'also', and ستم = 'oppression/injustice'. The clause contrasts that from him they witnessed both justice and also injustice.
segments.s321-g25: فرازنده is an agent noun/participle 'one who raises/exalts' placed before its objects; Persian often lists the objects after the agent joined by و. There is no finite verb here—this is a nominal phrase describing someone who lifts 'نیزه و تیغ و اسب' (spear, sword, steed).
segments.s321-g26: فروزنده is an adjectival/participial epithet 'radiant/illuminating' modifying the proper name آذرگشسب; فرخ is another epithet 'fortunate'. The sequence is noun-modifier + epithets + name, a common poetic apposition.
segments.s321-g27: نترسی is a negative jussive/imperative (2nd person singular) meaning 'do not fear'. ز introduces the source/thing feared ('رنج' = hardship). و links the next negative command ننازی, here 'do not be coy/boast' (negative imperative). به + گنج marks the object/target of the boasting 'about treasure'.
segments.s321-g28: به گیتی is a locative phrase 'in the world' (به + گیتی). ز here marks comparison/source 'from/than' your treasure (گنجت). فزونست is an archaic fused third-person present 'فزون است' meaning 'is more/greater'. The clause literally reads 'In the world, from your treasure greater is hardship.'
segments.s321-g29: همه پهلوانان is the subject 'all the champions'. ترا appears as the clitic object 'you' split into ت + را (object marker) — an older/poetic orthography. بنده‌ایم is the predicate 'we are (your) servants' with first-person plural present copula.
segments.s321-g30: سراسر is an adverb 'entirely/throughout' modifying the following phrase. به دیدار تو is a prepositional phrase 'at the sight/meeting of you' (literally 'to your sight'), and زنده‌ایم is the first-person plural present 'we are alive' — together: 'at your sight we are wholly alive.'
segments.s321-g31: همه دشمنان را is the definite object ('all the enemies' marked by را) placed before the verb for emphasis. سپردی is simple past, second-person singular 'you handed/consigned'. به خاک is a directional/adverbial phrase 'to the ground/earth' (i.e. cast to the dust).
segments.s321-g32: نماندت is an older past negative form (2nd person) meaning 'you did not leave' / 'there did not remain (for you)'. به گیتی 'in the world' (به + گیتی). ز کس marks 'because of/from anyone' (i.e. caused by anyone). بیم و باک are two synonymous nouns 'fear and dread' joined by و; the whole clause says you left no fear/dread in the world on account of anyone.
segments.s321-g65: This is a prepositional time/location phrase in archaic word order: 'به یک هفته ... پیش' = 'a week ago'; 'پیش یزدان' can be read as 'before/in the presence of God', and 'به پای' is the idiom 'at the feet (of)', i.e. 'before'. The personal pronoun من is placed between the time phrase and the place for emphasis.
segments.s321-g66: ببودم is an archaic/past form meaning 'I was / I had been' (the old past used in epic poetry). 'به اندیشه' = 'in thought' and 'پاک‌رای' is a noun/adjective meaning 'of pure/virtuous mind'; the conjunction و links the two predicates describing the speaker's state.
segments.s321-g67: The noun phrase 'یکی آرزو' = 'one/a single wish' (یکی as indefinite/numeral). دارم is the present first‑person 'I have'. 'اندر نهان' is a postpositional phrase meaning 'in secret / hidden', with اندر = 'in/within' and نهان = 'hidden'.
segments.s321-g68: همی is an archaic verbal particle that gives habitual or emphatic force and commonly appears before verbs in classical Persian. خواهم here is first person singular expressing desire or future action ('I will / I wish'), and از + کردگار جهان marks the source/recipient of the request: 'of the Creator of the world'. The object of خواهم (the wish) is the previous 'یکی آرزو'.
segments.s321-g69: بگویم is a first‑person verbal form meaning 'I will say / I shall speak' (classical usage). گشاده modifies بگویم as 'openly/frankly'. چو here functions as a conditional 'if/when' linking to the subordinate clause 'پاسخ دهید' ('you give/offer an answer' — دهيد is 2nd plural present/imperative).
segments.s321-g70: This clause is a request/imperative toward the addressees. 'به پاسخ مرا' = 'in/for my answer' (مرا = 'my' + را = object marker, here written split), 'روز فرخ' = 'a fortunate/auspicious day' (روز = day, فرخ = fortunate), and نهید (from نهاد) is a 2nd‑person plural imperative/subjunctive 'set / grant / place'. Literally: 'Make/set a fortunate day for my answer.'
segments.s321-g71: شما is the 2nd‑person plural/formal 'you'. 'پیش یزدان' = 'before/in the presence of God' (پیش with the meaning 'in the presence of'). نیایش کنید is an exhortation: نیایش (prayer) + کنید (2nd‑person plural hortative/imperative of کردن), 'perform prayer' or 'pray'.
segments.s321-g72: بر این کام و شادی introduces the object/occasion of praise: بر = 'on/for/regarding', این کام و شادی = 'this desire and joy'. ستایش کنید repeats the hortative verb 'offer praise' (ستایش + کنید). The structure is a paired exhortation: pray and praise with respect to the stated wish and joy.
segments.s321-g17: چو here is the comparative/simile particle 'like/as'. The clause lists names in parallel (چو X و چو Y ...) with the adjective دلیر after گیو as a postpositive attribute ('Giv the brave'), which is normal in Persian. The repeated چو creates a series of similes rather than a temporal sense.
segments.s321-g18: چو again = 'like/as'. گرگین and بیژن are coordinated by و. The final چو before رهام marks another simile and شیر is an epithet ('a lion') placed after رهام as a postpositive noun used adjectivally — no copula is expressed.
segments.s321-g19: چو here is temporal 'when' followed by the past verb دیدند ('they saw'). بردند is past plural 'they carried/took/brought' and نماز is the object; پیشش is a locative 'before/in the presence of him'. Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect (object before prepositional location).
segments.s321-g20: از آن پس is an idiom 'after that/from then on'. همه is the subject 'everyone/all'. برگشادند is past plural from برگشودن 'they opened/revealed' and راز is the direct object 'the secret'. The verb precedes its object here (poetic inversion).
segments.s321-g21: که introduces the following clause ('that'). شاها is a vocative 'O king'. A string of epithets follows without an explicit copula (typical in poetic Persian): دلیرا 'brave', گوا 'witness', داورا 'judge' — nouns used predicatively after the vocative.
segments.s321-g22: جهاندار is another epithet 'world-ruler/sovereign'. و simply coordinates. بر governs the following plural (مهتران) meaning 'over/above the chiefs'; مهترا is a postpositive epithet giving a superlative/illustrious nuance — an honorific placed after the noun.
segments.s321-g23: چو = 'when' introduces a temporal clause. تو شاه is a vocative/addressing 'you, O king'. ننشست is a past narrative form 'sat down' — the initial ن is a prosthetic letter often used in classical Persian for metrical reasons; the verb appears in 3rd singular narrative form even though تو is vocative, a common epic/narrative stylistic pattern. بر تخت عاج = 'upon the ivory throne'.
segments.s321-g24: فروغ (radiance/splendor) is the subject; از تو گیرد is a source phrase 'takes from you' with گیرد present 3rd-singular 'takes' (here a habitual/general present). همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle marking continuous or habitual action. مهر و تاج are coordinated objects 'love/favor and crown'.
segments.s321-g81: همه پهلوانان is the subject: a plural noun phrase 'all the champions/warriors'. ز نزدیک شاه is a prepositional phrase where ز (poetic 'from') governs نزدیک شاه ('near the king'), producing 'from/away from near the king.' Word order is inverted poetry but functions as a location/source adjunct for the verb in the next segment.
segments.s321-g82: برون آمدند is the past plural verb 'they went out / they came forth.' از غمان is 'from/out of sorrows' where از marks the source or cause; جان تباه is a noun + adjective 'life ruined' (the possessive 'their' is implicit from the subject). Together this gives a causal/conditional sense: they went out, their lives ruined by grief.
segments.s321-g83: به سالار بار is the indirect object marked by به: 'to the chamberlain/chief steward' (سالار بار is a compound/title). آن زمان is a temporal adverbial 'at that time.' گفت شاه places the verb 'said' before the subject 'the king' — a common poetic inversion: 'the king said.'
segments.s321-g84: که introduces the reported command ('that' / introducing the subordinate clause). بنشین is the 2nd-person imperative 'sit down.' پس پرده بارگاه is a locative phrase: پس 'after/behind' + پرده 'curtain' + بارگاه 'the palace/royal hall' — 'behind the palace curtain.'
segments.s321-g85: کسی را: 'anyone' as the (definite-marked) object (را marks the object). مده is the negative imperative 'do not give.' بار here means 'a burden/charge/office' (a responsibility to be assigned). در پیش من='in my presence' (در پیش = before/in front of; من = me). The whole clause is an imperative addressed to the chamberlain.
segments.s321-g86: ز introduces source/origin 'from.' بیگانه 'a stranger/foreigner.' و 'and.' مردم خویش من is 'my own people': مردم 'people,' خوی + ش is the root + possessive suffix (khvish = 'own/his'), and من repeats/strongly marks the possessor 'my' (an emphatic/poetic doubleness).
segments.s321-g87: بیامد is the past tense 'he came.' به جای پرستش is a locative/complement 'to the place of worship' (جای = place, پرستش = worship). به شب places the time 'at night' (به used here for temporal 'at'). The subject is implicit (he/one previously mentioned).
segments.s321-g88: به دادار دارنده is a prepositional phrase 'to the Creator, the Possessor' — two epithets in apposition. بگشاد is the narrative/past-form verb 'he opened' (poetic form of گشودن); لب is the object 'lip(s)' — 'he opened his lips' (i.e., he began to speak/pray).
segments.s321-g57: ز دشمن is a prepositional phrase (classical 'ز' = modern 'از') meaning 'from/against the enemy' placed before the clause for emphasis. چو introduces a simile ('as/like') and links کین پدر ('the father's vengeance' = 'vengeance for my father') to the speaker's action; خواستم is the 1st‑person past tense ('I wanted/sought'). Note the archaic 'ز' (poetic form of 'از') and the verb-final placement typical of Classical Persian poetry.
segments.s321-g58: This clause has two instrumental prepositional phrases be + noun ('به داد و به دین' = 'by/with justice and by/with religion') followed by the object 'گیتی' and the 1st‑person past verb 'آراستم' ('I adorned'). Here به functions as an instrumental 'with/by', and آراستم is the past tense, 1st‑person singular form meaning 'I adorned/embellished'.
segments.s321-g59: به گیتی is a locative/prepositional phrase ('in the world'); پی خاک تیره is a nominal phrase meaning 'a trace/patch of dark dust/earth' (پی = 'track/trace'), and نماند is the 3rd‑person past negative ('there remained not' / 'did not remain'). The word order places the locative and noun phrase before the verb, as is common in poetic Persian.
segments.s321-g60: که is the subordinating conjunction 'that'. مهر نگین مرا is a compact noun phrase: مهر here is best read as 'seal' (a stamp/signet) and نگین 'signet/gem', with مرا representing the enclitic 'me/my' (here written split in the manuscript). برنخواند is a 3rd‑person past negative form meaning 'did not call/summon' (or 'did not read aloud' in other contexts); context makes 'did not summon/call' the right sense. Note that the enclitic مرا is here split orthographically into separate tokens in the edition.
segments.s321-g61: شما is the 2nd‑person plural/polite 'you'. تیغها (swords) is the direct object, در نیام is a locative phrase 'into the scabbard', and آورید is an imperative/command form addressed to 'you' (2nd‑person plural) meaning 'bring/put' (here: 'put into' / 'sheath'). Word order places subject/pronoun and object before the verb, typical of Persian commands in classical style.
segments.s321-g62: This is another imperative clause: a coordinated noun phrase 'می‌سرخ و سیمینه جام' ('red wine and a silvery cup') is followed by the same imperative آورید 'bring'. می‌سرخ is a fused adjective+substantive (literally 'wine-red' = 'red wine'); سیمینه is an adjective 'silvery', and جام is 'cup'.
segments.s321-g63: به جای is a fixed prepositional expression meaning 'in place of' / 'instead of'. چرنگ modifies کمان (an adjectival premodifier meaning 'withered/gaunt' in this poetic context) and the phrase lists the alternative instruments نای و چنگ ('ney (reed‑flute) and harp'). Word order places the replacement items after the prepositional phrase and before the verb.
segments.s321-g64: بسازید is the 2nd‑person plural imperative/subjunctive 'make/construct/compose'. با introduces the means/instrument ('with') and is followed by a triad of qualities or accompaniments: باده (wine), بوی (scent), و رنگ (and color). The clause instructs to fashion the instruments using these elements; note the classical imperative and the instrumental با.
segments.s321-g41: بگوید is a third-person present-subjunctive/jussive used here as a polite imperative 'let him tell'. به ما marks the indirect object 'to us'. تا introduces purpose ('so that'), دلش is 'his heart' (دل + enclitic ش = his), and خوش کنیم is a first-person-plural subjunctive 'we may make (it) glad' — the two verbs بگوید and کنـیم are linked by the purpose clause.
segments.s321-g42: This is a coordinated predication with the same final verb کنيم ('we will make/ do'). پر از ... کنيم is a causative phrase 'we will make (them) full of ...'; here two objects are given in parallel (دل and رخ): پر (full/of) + از marks the 'full of' relation, خون دل is a poetic compound 'blood of the heart' (intense grief), and رخ پر آتش means 'face full of fire' (fiery appearance or anger) — the clause states what they will fill/make full.
segments.s321-g43: وگر is a conditional 'and if' (classical/poetic). دشمنی دارد is a present-tense statement 'he has enmity' (دارد = 'has/possesses'), and اندر نهان is an adverbial phrase 'in secret/hiddenly' (اندر = 'in/within', نهان = 'hidden/secret'). The clause describes a possible concealed hostility.
segments.s321-g44: بگوید is again the third-person subjunctive/jussive used as an exhortation 'let him tell' or as an address to the ruler to have it told. به ما 'to us' is the indirect object. شهریار جهان follows the verb as a vocative/title addressing the king ('O sovereign of the world'), so the line reads as a request to the ruler to be informed.
segments.s321-g45: همه تاجداران is 'all the crown-bearers' (i.e. crowned rulers). که introduces a relative clause 'who', so که بودند شاه is 'who were kings' with بودند as the past/plural copula 'were' and شاه the predicate 'kings'; the whole phrase defines the class being spoken of.
segments.s321-g46: بدین داشتند is a compact classical expression meaning 'by/with this they had' or 'thus they possessed' (بدین = 'by this/thus', داشتند = 'they had'). What follows — ارج گنج و سپاه — are the items they possessed: 'honor, treasure, and an army' listed as direct objects of داشتند.
segments.s321-g47: که‌گر is a conditional 'if/whenever' (classical), introducing an either/or clause. سر ستانند 'they take heads' uses ستانند (present subjunctive/plural) meaning 'seize/remove' (here of heads), و‌گر 'or if' repeats the condition for the alternative, and سر دهند 'they give (up) heads' (دهند = 'they give' — idiomatically 'suffer beheading' or 'offer heads'). The verbs are general/plural and express alternative outcomes.
segments.s321-g48: چو = 'when/as'. ترگ is a simile 'rag/shred' and modifies how the heads are treated. دلیران is the genitive/possessive/plural 'of the brave' or 'brave men'. به سر برنهند is a transitive construction 'they place upon the head' (به marking the target 'on/upon', برنهند = 'place on/top of'), so the clause describes placing the brave men's heads upon (something/others' heads) as if they were rags — a vivid, proverbial image.

sentences.s321-s25: If he has a secret to tell us, let him seek that very remedy from us.
sentences.s321-s26: Then the king said to them thus: that you have no one to fight with.
sentences.s321-s27: I suffer no harm from the enemy in the world, nor was there anywhere treasure scattered.
sentences.s321-s28: I have no harm from the army's campaign, nor among you is there a guilty man.
sentences.s321-s45: He kept saying, "O you higher than high, increaser of purity and preeminence."
sentences.s321-s46: Be thou my guide to the heaven, that I may pass away from this spongy dwelling.
sentences.s321-s47: My heart did nothing — it found nothing; (the) soul’s bright place (is) for hearts set apart.
sentences.s321-s5: On that day of battle each of those famed men thought otherwise.
sentences.s321-s6: When the famed sovereign mounted the throne, he came into the presence of the chief of the baggage.
sentences.s321-s7: He ordered; they removed the curtain, and they left the army outside the court.
sentences.s321-s8: They departed, having put hands to their weapons; the great chiefs — elephant-throwers and lion-crushers.
sentences.s321-s17: In every realm your army and treasure are present; wherever you go, suffering follows you.
sentences.s321-s18: We do not know the king's intention—why things have grown gloomy in these times.
sentences.s321-s19: A day of parting from this world will befall you; it is not a time for care and for withering.
sentences.s321-s20: If the king causes us any hurt, the blame for his harming us is not on us.
sentences.s321-s1: For a week, day and night his body remained there, while his soul was in another place.
sentences.s321-s2: When the week ended, Khosrow was renewed, but he no longer had the strength to remain in the place of worship.
sentences.s321-s3: On the eighth day he left the place of worship, went to the royal throne, and strode proudly.
sentences.s321-s4: All the champions of Iran — the army — were left astonished at the king's deed.
sentences.s321-s37: Who established a system of judgment for good and evil; praise (be) from me to him who showed the way.
sentences.s321-s38: From that time on, make merry for me; free my spirit from evils and make it without doubt.
sentences.s321-s39: Know that this unstable wheel does not always keep (anyone) beneath the king.
sentences.s321-s40: Ever did old and young part together; from him we saw justice, and from him also oppression.
sentences.s321-s13: The raiser of spear and sword and steed — the radiant, fortunate Azar-Gashasp.
sentences.s321-s14: Do not fear hardship, and do not boast of treasure; in the world, hardship is greater than your treasure.
sentences.s321-s15: All champions are your servants; at the sight of you we are wholly alive.
sentences.s321-s16: You consigned all the enemies to the earth; you left no fear or dread of anyone in the world.
sentences.s321-s33: A week ago I stood at God's feet, in thought and with a pure mind.
sentences.s321-s34: I have one wish hidden in secret; I will ask it of the Creator of the world.
sentences.s321-s35: I will speak plainly; if you answer, grant my reply a fortunate day.
sentences.s321-s36: Pray before God; for this wish and joy, offer praise.
sentences.s321-s9: Like Tūs and like Goudarz, and Giv the brave; like Gorgin and Bijan, and Roham like a lion.
sentences.s321-s10: When they saw him, they brought (or offered) prayer before him; after that all revealed the secret.
sentences.s321-s11: that, O king, (you are) brave, a witness, a judge, a ruler of the world, and above the chiefs most eminent.
sentences.s321-s12: When you, O king, sat on the ivory throne, splendor would always take (from you) love and crown.
sentences.s321-s41: All the champions left the king's vicinity; their lives ruined by sorrow.
sentences.s321-s42: At that time the king said to the chamberlain: 'Sit behind the palace curtain.'
sentences.s321-s43: Do not give anyone a charge before me — neither from strangers nor from my own people.
sentences.s321-s44: He came at night to the place of worship; before the Creator, the Possessor, he opened his lips.
sentences.s321-s29: I sought vengeance on the enemy as for my father; I adorned the world with justice and religion.
sentences.s321-s30: No dark patch of earth remained in the world that did not call my signet.
sentences.s321-s31: You, sheath your swords in their scabbards; bring red wine and a silvery cup.
sentences.s321-s32: Instead of the withered bow, make a ney and a harp; make them with wine, scent and color.
sentences.s321-s21: Let him tell us so that we may make his heart glad; we will fill our hearts with blood and set our faces ablaze.
sentences.s321-s22: And if he harbors enmity in secret, tell us, O sovereign of the world.
sentences.s321-s23: All the crown-bearers who were kings thus possessed honor, treasure and an army.
sentences.s321-s24: Whether they take heads or give them up, when they set the brave men's heads upon (others') heads like rags.
